articles.author: Jon Gambrell, Michelle L. Price, Julie Watson
articles.description: President Donald Trump says he is considering “winding down” military operations in the Middle East. Trump's social media post comes as the U.S. is sending three more amphibious assault ships and roughly 2,500 additional Marines to the region. The mixed messages come after oil prices climbed yet again, putting pressure on the U.S. stock market. That was followed by a Trump administration announcement that it will lift sanctions on Iranian oil loaded on ships. The move aims to wrangle soaring fuel prices. The three-week-old war is showing no signs of letting up, with Israel saying Iran continued to fire missiles at it early Saturday.
articles.title: Trump hints at wind-down as US sends more troops to Middle East

articles.author: Nicholas Riccardi
articles.description: President Donald Trump is sending mixed messages about the U.S. war with Iran, and that's raising even more questions about the direction of the conflict and his administration's strategy. Trump says he's considering winding down the war and suggesting other countries could police the strategically vital Strait of Hormuz. Yet also on Friday, administration officials said the United States was sending more warships and about 2,500 more Marines to the Middle East. And the U.S. is easing sanctions on some Iranian oil that's already at sea, in a move aimed at lowering energy prices. The war remains on an unpredictable path and a credible endgame is unclear.
articles.title: Trump delivers mixed messages about the war with Iran

articles.author: Lisa Mascaro
articles.description: President Donald Trump took the United States to war without a vote of support from Congress. Now, lawmakers are increasingly questioning when, how and at what cost the war with Iran will come to an end. Under the War Powers Act, the president can engage in military operations for 60 days without approval from Congress. As that deadline approaches, some lawmakers want more information on the goals and an exit plan. Meanwhile, a $200 billion request from the Pentagon for war funds is circulating, oil prices are spiking and thousands of U.S. troops are deploying to the Middle East.
articles.title: Congress looks for Trump's exit plan as the Iran war drags on

articles.author: Jill Lawless
articles.description: Iran has launched missiles toward Diego Garcia, a remote Indian Ocean island that hosts a major U.K.-U.S. military base. The missiles failed to hit the base which is about 2,500 miles or 4,000 kilometers from Iran. The base has supported U.S. operations across the Middle East, Asia and Africa for decades. Britain owns the island and says U.S. forces can use the base to strike Iranian missile sites. That has drawn threats from Iran. The base sits in the disputed Chagos Islands. A deal for Britain to hand sovereignty over the islands to Mauritius has been stalled by opposition from U.S. President Donald Trump.
articles.title: What to know about Diego Garcia after Iran targeted the US base

articles.author: Collin Binkley
articles.description: President Donald Trump says he'll order federal immigration officers to take a role soon in airport security unless Democrats agree on a bill to fund the Department of Homeland Security. In a pair of social media posts, Trump first threatened and then said he'd made plans to put officers from U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement in airports beginning Monday if the congressional standoff continues. He made the announcement as a partial shutdown contributes to long lines to get through screening at some of the nation’s largest airports. The president suggested ICE agents would bring the administration’s immigration crackdown into the nation’s airports.
articles.title: Trump says ICE will work airport security unless Democrats fund DHS

articles.author: David Bauder
articles.description: A judge this week ruled that Voice of America's employees — and the news organization itself — get back to work. Whether that actually happens is anybody's guess. The government-run agency that broadcasts news around the world has been limping along with a skeleton staff for the past year, largely shut down by the Trump administration. But twice in the past two weeks, U.S. District Court Judge Royce C. Lamberth has ruled in favor of lawsuits designed to keep it alive, saying that its overseer didn't have the authority to take the actions she did, and that employees placed on paid leave should return to work.
articles.title: Can Voice of America be put back together again?

articles.author: Garance Burke
articles.description: Nearly 600 immigrant children were held in a Texas family detention center in recent months beyond court-mandated limits and without enough food, medical care or mental health services, with dozens languishing inside for more than three months, according to court documents filed Friday. Children and families held in the Dilley detention facility where 5-year-old Liam Conejo Ramos and his father were sent earlier this year faced virus outbreaks and lasting lockdowns, per the attorney’s reports. The case of Ramos stirred protest over the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown, including among detainees behind Dilley’s chain-link fences, and the number of children detained there has fallen in recent weeks.
articles.title: Detained immigrant children still face concerning conditions, lawyers say

articles.author: Eric Tucker
articles.description: Robert S. Mueller III, the FBI director who transformed the nation’s premier law enforcement agency into a terrorism-fighting force after the Sept. 11, 2001, attacks and who later became special counsel in charge of investigating ties between Russia and Donald Trump’s presidential campaign, has died. He was 81. His family says in a statement that Mueller died Friday night.
articles.title: Former FBI Director Robert Mueller dies

articles.description: Cuba’s Ministry of Energy and Mines says a total disconnection of the National Electric System has occurred, marking the second nationwide blackout reported in a week. That's according to a statement posted on social media Saturday. The ministry says protocols to restore electricity service across the country are already being implemented.
articles.title: Cuba reports second nationwide power grid outage in a week

articles.author: Foster Klug, Mari Yamaguchi
articles.description: High level officials usually shy away from public discussion of Japan’s 1941 sneak attack on U.S. forces at Pearl Harbor. So there was embarrassment, confusion and unease after President Donald Trump used the World War II attack to justify his secrecy before launching the war against Iran. The awkward Japanese reaction was compounded by the fact that Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi was sitting awkwardly at Trump’s side. It also reveals a broader discomfort here at how Japan talks about its responsibility in World War II, even 80 years after its end.
articles.title: Surprise, unease in Japan after Trump uses Pearl Harbor to defend Iran war
